elements.sent-2411-001: We develop a systematic approach to calculate the contribution of edge states to the thermal Hall conductivity and energy magnetization.
elements.sent-2411-002: A two-dimensional topological insulator in equilibrium at a finite temperature is analyzed in the presence of a static gravitational field, which mimics the variations in the temperature distribution.
elements.sent-2411-003: From this model, an effective boundary free energy functional for the gravitational field is derived.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our analysis reveals that, unlike in the case of massive Dirac fermions, the addition of the Newtonian mass term in the topological insulator model significantly modifies the quantum anomalous behavior of the boundary states, leading to a non-zero bulk thermal current.
elements.sent-2411-005: This additional contribution violates the Wiedemann-Franz law.
elements.sent-2411-006: There has been a growing experimental interest in investigating the thermal Hall effect in different systems, including multiferroics~\cite{ideue2017giant , topological insulators~\cite{shimizu2015quantum , quantum magnets~\cite{zhang2024thermal , and various other materials~\cite{grissonnanche2019giant,samajdar2019enhanced,fulga2020temperature,grissonnanche2020chiral .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: This interest stems from its potential to reveal topological properties that are inaccessible through conventional Hall effects, probe invariants in systems with broken $U(1)$ symmetry~\cite{golan2018probing , with neutral excitations~\cite{grissonnanche2020chiral , and uncover complex boundary state structures in strongly correlated systems~\cite{kane1997quantized .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Significant theoretical effort has been put into the microscopic description and study of this phenomenon~\cite{cooper1997thermoelectric,qin2011energy,luttinger1964theory,shitade2014heat,paul2003thermal,cappelli2002thermal,bradlyn2015low,stone2012gravitational,nomura2012cross .}
elements.sent-2411-009: From a microscopic perspective~\cite{luttinger1964theory,qin2011energy,cooper1997thermoelectric , thermal responses can be calculated using Luttinger's “trick”, which exploits the equivalence in the linear-response regime between a non-flat metric tensor (also referred to as a “gravitational potential”, in analogy to the electric potential) and temperature fluctuations that generate energy currents.}
elements.sent-2411-010: From an effective theory perspective~\cite{stone2012gravitational,bradlyn2015low,cappelli2002thermal,read2000paired , it is argued that, based on the existence of gapless edge states, the thermal Hall conductivity $\kappa_{xy}$ of a topological insulator at low temperatures is determined by the central charge of the edge theory~\cite{kane1997quantized .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: As to the microscopic perspective, previous theories have often assumed scaling relations for heat currents without providing any microscopic justification.
elements.sent-2411-013: Moreover, there remains some ambiguity in how to extend these methods to interacting systems~\cite{qin2011energy,shitade2014heat .}
elements.sent-2411-014: These shortcomings make the microscopic approach not fully satisfactory.
elements.sent-2411-015: On the other hand, the effective action approach describes the edge states by means of a conformal field theory, which is in turn based on an underlying low-energy topological field theory, i.e. the gravitational Chern-Simons theory~\cite{stone2012gravitational,bradlyn2015low .}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, deriving the gravitational Chern-Simons theory directly from a microscopic bulk Hamiltonian poses significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, it would be preferable to derive the edge theory (and its associated thermal Hall conductivity) directly from the bulk microscopic Hamiltonian, without relying on any topological field theory.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we derive a long-wavelength description of edge states from a bulk microscopic Hamiltonian without assuming Lorentz symmetry, which is in general broken in a topological insulator.
elements.sent-2411-019: As a consequence, the energy current cannot be simply defined as the functional derivative of the Hamiltonian with respect to the vielbein field.
elements.sent-2411-020: To resolve this, we introduce an additional source term in the Hamiltonian and refer to it as ``extended Hamiltonian''.
elements.sent-2411-021: Because the extra source term vanishes when the metric reduces to Luttinger’s one, the extended Hamiltonian can still be used to define the partition function.
elements.sent-2411-022: Compared to previous approaches~\cite{nakai2016finite,liu2024no , this method can be easily generalized to treat systems that are not massive Dirac fermions.}
elements.sent-2411-023: From the study of a (2+1)-dimensional topological insulator model, we further demonstrate that the introduction of the Newtonian mass term~\cite{qi2011topological significantly modifies the quantum anomalous behavior of the edge states.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In contrast to massive Dirac fermions~\cite{liu2024no , in a generic topological insulator the presence of a Newtonian mass term~\cite{qi2011topological breaks energy conservation when the metric is allowed to vary in space.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: This suggests that a bulk thermal Hall current can exist even when the gradient of the gravitational field is uniform.
elements.sent-2411-026: This conclusion cannot be explained by the gravitational Chern-Simons action, which can be microscopically derived from the (2+1)-dimensional massive Dirac fermion model.
elements.sent-2411-027: In the latter, the thermal Hall current is proportional to higher derivatives of the metric.
elements.sent-2411-028: Additionally, the bulk thermal Hall response introduces an extra contribution to the thermal Hall conductivity proportional to $T^3$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Therefore, the Wiedemann-Franz law only holds in the low-temperature limit.

elements.sent-2411-132: We study a two-dimensional topological insulator in the presence of a static non-uniform gravitational field, which mimics the variations in the temperature distribution.
elements.sent-2411-133: We derive an effective boundary free energy functional for the gravitational field and show that, in contrast to the case of massive Dirac fermions, the addition of a Newtonian mass term significantly modifies the quantum anomalous behavior of the system.
elements.sent-2411-134: A non-zero bulk thermal current appears, which violates the Wiedemann-Franz law.
elements.sent-2411-135: The systematic approach we develop to calculate the contribution of edge states to thermal Hall conductivity and energy magnetization can easily be extended to other models.
elements.sent-2411-136: In fact, it enables probing invariants in systems with broken $U(1)$ symmetry~\cite{golan2018probing , with neutral excitations~\cite{grissonnanche2020chiral , and uncovering complex boundary state structures in strongly correlated systems~\cite{kane1997quantized .}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: In the thermal Hall effect, heat currents are deflected perpendicular to the temperature gradient.
elements.sent-2411-138: The phenomenon is analogous to the electrical Hall effect but with heat flow in place of charge currents.
elements.sent-2411-139: Experimental evidence for the thermal Hall effect has been steadily growing in various systems, including multiferroics~\cite{ideue2017giant , topological insulators~\cite{shimizu2015quantum , quantum magnets~\cite{zhang2024thermal , and various other materials~\cite{grissonnanche2019giant,samajdar2019enhanced,fulga2020temperature,grissonnanche2020chiral .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: From a microscopic perspective~\cite{luttinger1964theory,qin2011energy,cooper1997thermoelectric , thermal responses can be calculated using Luttinger's “trick”~\cite{luttinger1964theory .}}
elements.sent-2411-141: This exploits the equivalence in the linear-response regime between a non-flat metric tensor (also termed ``gravitational potential'' in analogy to the electric one) and temperature gradients that generate energy currents.
elements.sent-2411-142: From an effective theory perspective~\cite{stone2012gravitational,bradlyn2015low,cappelli2002thermal,read2000paired the thermal Hall conductivity $\kappa_{xy}$ of a topological insulator at low temperatures is determined by the central charge of the corresponding edge theory~\cite{kane1997quantized .}}
elements.sent-2411-143: Both approaches have limitations that make them not fully satisfactory.
elements.sent-2411-144: Within the microscopic perspective, scaling relations for heat currents have often been assumed without providing any microscopic justification.
elements.sent-2411-145: Moreover, ambiguities remain about how to extend these methods to interacting systems~\cite{qin2011energy,shitade2014heat .}
elements.sent-2411-146: On the other hand, the effective action approach describes edge states by means of a conformal field theory, based on an underlying low-energy topological field theory (i.e. the gravitational Chern-Simons theory~\cite{stone2012gravitational,bradlyn2015low ).}
elements.sent-2411-147: However, deriving the gravitational Chern-Simons theory directly from a microscopic bulk Hamiltonian poses significant challenges~\cite{sumiyoshi2013quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-148: Therefore, it would be preferable to derive the conformal edge theory (and its associated thermal Hall conductivity) directly from the bulk microscopic Hamiltonian, without relying on any topological field theory as an intermediate step.
elements.sent-2411-149: In this paper, we carry out this precise task.
elements.sent-2411-150: We derive a long-wavelength description of edge states from a bulk microscopic Hamiltonian without assuming Lorentz symmetry, which is in general broken in a topological insulator.
elements.sent-2411-151: To resolve this, we introduce an additional source term in the Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-152: Because this term vanishes when the metric reduces to Luttinger’s one, this ``extended Hamiltonian'' can be used to define the partition function.
elements.sent-2411-153: Compared to previous approaches~\cite{nakai2016finite,liu2024no , this method allows one to treat systems that are not massive Dirac fermions.}
elements.sent-2411-154: From the study of a (2+1)-dimensional topological insulator model, we demonstrate that the broken Lorentz symmetry significantly modifies the quantum anomalous behavior of edge states.
elements.sent-2411-155: This result cannot be obtained from the gravitational Chern-Simons action, which can only be derived from a (2+1)-dimensional massive Dirac fermion model.
elements.sent-2411-156: In the latter model, the bulk thermal Hall current is proportional to higher derivatives of the metric.
elements.sent-2411-157: Furthermore, we find that the thermal Hall conductivity contains a term proportional to $T^3$ which cannot be obtained from the gravitational Chern-Simons theory.
elements.sent-2411-158: We conclude that the Wiedemann-Franz law only holds in the low-temperature limit.
